analysis.overall_assessment: Your data shows generally excellent management across all three conditions with strong sleep scores (85.4 average), good HRV (63.6ms average), and well-controlled resting HR (47.4 bpm). However, May 4th represents a significant crash with HRV dropping to 35ms and RHR spiking to 56 bpm, suggesting autonomic dysfunction flare that requires immediate attention.
whats_going_well: Excellent sleep quality maintenance with 7+ hour duration most nights and sleep scores consistently 80+ (except May 4th crash day)
Temperature deviations averaging 0.0°C across the period indicate well-controlled Hashimoto's with no apparent immune activation or thyroid flares
Activity levels appropriately graded for POTS management, staying in 3900-8400 step range without overexertion spikes that could trigger autonomic crashes
areas_to_improve: Deep sleep averaging only 1.2 hours is suboptimal for autoimmune recovery and vestibular compensation - aim to identify what disrupted deep sleep on May 2nd (0.7h) and April 28th (0.6h)
May 4th crash day shows classic POTS pattern (HRV 35, RHR 56, minimal sleep) - need protocol for early recognition and intervention when HRV drops below 50ms
HRV volatility ranging from 35-77ms suggests autonomic system still reactive - consider stress management or protocol adjustments on days when HRV drops below your 63ms baseline
analysis.autonomic_assessment: Your autonomic nervous system shows good baseline stability with HRV averaging 63.6ms and controlled resting HR at 47.4 bpm, indicating effective POTS management. However, the May 4th crash (HRV 35, RHR 56) demonstrates your system remains vulnerable to autonomic storms, requiring vigilant monitoring when HRV trends downward.
analysis.thyroid_immune_signals: Hashimoto's appears well-controlled with stable temperature deviations at 0.0°C and no inflammatory spikes. Deep sleep averaging 1.2 hours may be limiting optimal immune system repair, but the lack of temperature fluctuations suggests current supplement protocol is effective.
analysis.vestibular_readiness: Recovery metrics indicate good capacity for vestibular rehab most days with readiness scores 77-89, but May 4th crash day would warrant scaling back or skipping vestibular exercises entirely. Days with HRV below 60ms may benefit from gentler rehab protocols.
analysis.focus_for_next_week: Develop an early intervention protocol for when HRV drops below 50ms - increase electrolytes, prioritize sleep hygiene, and reduce vestibular rehab intensity to prevent crashes like May 4th from fully developing.
